topic: Using Honcho (Plastic Labs) as the identity/representation layer in a hybrid agent-memory stack for OpenComputer v2
key_patterns: MEMORY-AS-REASONING, NOT RETRIEVAL: Honcho's core differentiator. You write raw messages/events; custom fine-tuned reasoning models run in the BACKGROUND (the Deriver) to extract explicit premises -> deductive -> inductive -> abductive conclusions, stored per-peer. You do NOT write 'facts' yourself; you write conversation/events and let Honcho derive the representation. README: 'Reasoning-first memory ... Extracts conclusions from conversations and events, not just matching chunks.' This is the opposite of an FTS/keyword index where you store atomic facts.

PEER / SESSION / WORKSPACE primitive model: Workspace = hard isolation boundary (one per app/tenant). Peer = any persistent entity (human OR agent OR project/idea) with ONE stable ID reused everywhere; splitting one identity across IDs (user-web vs user-discord) silently builds separate representations. Session = a bounded interaction (per-conversation/channel/task/import), many-to-many with peers. Identity lives on the peer, local context lives on the session.

REPRESENTATION = the per-peer accumulated reasoning (conclusions + summaries + peer card). It is queried two ways with a deliberate latency/quality tradeoff: peer.chat() (the Dialectic agentic endpoint, an LLM tool-loop, slower, synthesizes a natural-language answer to a specific question) vs peer.representation()/session.context(peer_target=...) (low-latency STATIC snapshot of cached conclusions, no LLM-in-the-loop, for prompt hydration and dashboards).

PEER CARD = compact, durable identity profile (name, occupation, standing instructions, preferences, traits) capped at 40 atomic string facts. This is the one place you SHOULD write directly (peer.set_card) to bootstrap known identity at signup or correct a wrong fact. It is auto-maintained by Dreaming. It is explicitly NOT for transient state (mood, current topic) — those stay in conclusions/summaries. set_card REPLACES the whole card (no merge).

THE DIALECTIC API as agent-to-agent oracle: peer.chat('Does the user prefer formal or casual tone?') returns a synthesized answer grounded in all reasoning. Best practice from the docs: expose it to your agent AS A TOOL and let the LLM formulate the query just-in-time; use specific questions ('What communication style?') not 'tell me about the user'; reasoning_level minimal->max trades latency for depth (default low).

THEORY-OF-MIND / DIRECTIONAL REPRESENTATIONS (observer->observed): internal collections are keyed by (observer, observed) pairs. Default is global self-representation (observer==observed). With observe_others=true at session level, peer A builds its OWN representation of peer B from only what A witnessed — enabling perspective/information-asymmetry (games, multi-agent, parent/subagent). Off by default; most single-user apps don't need it. observe_me=false turns OFF modeling of deterministic agents/bots (saves reasoning compute) while still storing their messages for session context.

ASYNC WRITE / BACKGROUND REASON / QUERY loop: add_messages stores immediately and returns (never block on reasoning). Reasoning is enqueued; representation/chat reads may lag a few seconds. Session-based queues preserve chronological ordering so a peer's representation updates in order. Newly-written content surfaces in keyword search instantly but in SEMANTIC search / chat only after background embedding+derivation.

TOKEN BATCHING write cadence: representation (conclusion) reasoning only fires once ~1,000 tokens of a peer's messages accumulate WITHIN A SINGLE SESSION. Short bursts ('ok','yes') wait in queue until the batch fills — nothing lost. Implication: don't fragment into many thin sessions; trickle/low-volume sources should append to ONE ongoing per-source session so they cross the threshold. Honcho bills per reasoning pass, so batching is also cost control.

DREAMING (offline consolidation): a scheduled/surprisal-prioritized background process that consolidates redundant/contradictory conclusions, derives deeper deductive+inductive conclusions, builds reasoning trees (premise->conclusion chains traversable via get_reasoning_chain), and refreshes peer cards. This is where contradiction-handling and 'insights never explicitly stated' come from. Toggleable per workspace/session.

INGESTION of non-chat data: emails, meeting notes, CRM records, documents (session.upload_file) are all just Messages attributed to the peer they're ABOUT (not to an agent), with created_at set to the EVENT time (not import time) so temporal reasoning is correct. This is how Honcho becomes the cross-source identity hub without you double-writing facts elsewhere.

INTERNAL hybrid search exists (BM25 + pgvector HNSW) and is exposed as peer/session/workspace .search(), but it returns raw MESSAGES, not conclusions. The conclusions surface is the Conclusions API + representation + chat. So Honcho already has FTS+vector internally over its OWN messages — which is exactly the overlap to avoid duplicating in an external store.
tradeoffs: Dialectic chat() quality vs latency: it's an agentic LLM tool-loop (search_memory, search_messages, get_observation_context, get_reasoning_chain, temporal tools), so a single call can take seconds and cost tokens. For hot-path prompt hydration prefer the static representation()/context() snapshot; reserve chat() for when the agent genuinely needs synthesized, query-specific reasoning.

Eventual consistency: async background reasoning means a just-stated preference is NOT immediately queryable via chat/semantic search (keyword search is instant). Good for write throughput, bad if you expect read-your-writes. Don't poll/block waiting for reasoning.

Reasoning quality requires volume: the ~1,000-token-per-session batching threshold means sparse or hyper-segmented usage produces little/no representation. You trade some immediacy/granularity for cost-efficient, meaningful batches.

Operational weight: Honcho needs Postgres(+pgvector) + Redis + a separate Deriver worker process + LLM provider keys. It is NOT a single-binary/edge/browser drop-in. For OC v2 (already running Honcho as a provider) this cost is paid, but it's real surface area.

Opinionated reasoning vs your control: Honcho deliberately reasons over EVERYTHING rather than letting you pick what to store ('our job is the most robust reasoning possible; your job as a developer is to decide what's relevant for your use case'). You get rich latent inference but less direct control over the canonical fact set — which is precisely why a separate authoritative graph/FTS store is complementary.

Directional representations (observe_others) add real power (perspective) but real complexity and cost, and are not retroactive (peers only reason over messages sent AFTER they join a session). Enable narrowly.
failure_modes: DOUBLE-STORING FACTS: writing the same atomic facts into Honcho's peer card AND an external graph/FTS store, then having them drift. Honcho should own DERIVED identity/preferences; the graph/FTS should own AUTHORITATIVE, queryable, structured facts (entities, relations, exact strings, code symbols, ticket IDs). They overlap if you push structured facts into Honcho expecting it to be a fact KB.

Treating Honcho as a keyword/fact lookup: it is NOT a general fact KB or exact-match index. Asking chat() for a precise stored value (an API key, a file path, an exact config) is the wrong tool — that belongs in FTS/SQLite/graph. chat() synthesizes, it doesn't guarantee verbatim recall.

Identity fragmentation: same user under multiple peer IDs (or per-host default workspaces) silently builds N separate representations and unifies nothing. The fix (one stable peer ID + one shared workspace across all hosts) is NOT the default — plugins default to per-host workspaces.

Thin-session stall: scoping a session per tiny message means each session never reaches the ~1,000-token batch threshold, so reasoning never fires and the representation stays empty — looks like 'Honcho isn't learning'.
Read-your-writes assumption: testing/asserting on chat() or semantic results immediately after add_messages fails because embedding+derivation is async. Tests must poll/wait or use keyword search.
peer.set_card overwrites: calling set_card with a partial list silently drops every previously-stored card fact (no merge), wiping bootstrapped identity.

Expecting retroactive perspective: adding an observer peer AFTER messages were sent yields no reasoning over that history (no retroactive scheduling) — the directional representation will be surprisingly empty.
Burning reasoning compute on deterministic agents: leaving observe_me=true on bots/tool-agents/NPCs wastes background LLM passes modeling behavior you fully control.
adopt: Make Honcho the IDENTITY/REPRESENTATION plane ONLY: it owns the per-user (and per-agent) representation — derived preferences, communication style, expertise level, goals, traits, theory-of-mind — and the peer card. Do not route exact-fact recall, code/doc search, or structured entity/relation queries through it. (This matches OC v2's existing 'triple memory' split: Honcho = identity/preferences, GBrain/graph = entities+relations, local markdown/FTS = exact recall.)

WRITE conversation turns and events, never pre-digested facts. Feed Honcho raw user/assistant turns (stripping tool_use blocks so only substantive content lands) and ingested events (emails, notes) attributed to the user peer with correct created_at. Let the Deriver produce the representation. This is the single most important rule to avoid double-storing facts that belong in the graph/FTS.

READ via the two-tier pattern: hydrate every agent prompt cheaply with session.context(peer_target=user) / peer.representation() (static, low-latency cached conclusions + peer card), and expose peer.chat() as an explicit AGENT TOOL (OC v2 already has honcho_reasoning/honcho_search/honcho_context) for just-in-time, query-specific theory-of-mind questions. Keep chat() off the synchronous hot path.

Use ONE stable peer ID per real user and ONE shared workspace across every OC v2 surface (web, CLI, Telegram, Discord, gateway) so the representation accumulates in one place — this is the 'unified memory' setup and is the fix for OC v2's history of stranded facts on stray peers. Derive peer IDs from immutable platform IDs, keep display names in metadata/peer card.

Set observe_me=false on deterministic agent/bot/tool peers (and OC v2 sub-agents you control) to save reasoning compute while still storing their messages for session context; reserve observe_others/directional representations for genuine multi-agent/perspective cases (e.g. agent personas modeling the user, or the Explore-Agents personas), not by default.

Bootstrap the peer card at known-identity moments (signup, profile edit, explicit user correction) with atomic, prefixed facts (INSTRUCTION:/PREFERENCE:/TRAIT:) and let Dreaming maintain it thereafter; remember set_card replaces — always re-include retained facts. Use the card for standing instructions the agent must never lose.

Scope sessions to the live interaction but NOT too thin: per-conversation/per-channel/per-task, and append trickle/low-volume ingestion (scheduled email/calendar/CRM jobs) to one ongoing per-source session so it crosses the ~1,000-token reasoning threshold.

Treat writes as fire-and-forget (async) and never block the agent on reasoning; for verification use honcho.queue_status() / keyword search rather than asserting on chat() immediately. Use the Dreaming/consolidation toggle and reasoning_level knobs to tune cost.

Define a CLEAN SEAM in OC v2 docs: Honcho = 'who the user is and how they like to be treated' (identity, preferences, theory-of-mind, standing instructions); graph (GBrain) = 'entities, relations, project/idea structure'; local FTS/SQLite = 'exact, verbatim, immediately-recallable facts (paths, keys, IDs, decisions)'. Route each new memory write to exactly one plane.
avoid: Do NOT use Honcho as a general fact knowledge base, keyword/exact-match index, or structured entity-relation store — it is a reasoning/representation layer; precise/verbatim/structured recall belongs in FTS/SQLite/graph.

Do NOT write atomic 'facts' into Honcho that also live in your graph/FTS (double-storing -> drift). Write raw messages/events; let Honcho derive. Keep the canonical fact set in the authoritative store.

Do NOT put exact-recall or structured queries through peer.chat() (it synthesizes, may not return verbatim, costs latency+tokens). Reserve chat() for fuzzy, identity/preference/theory-of-mind questions.

Do NOT fragment one user across multiple peer IDs or rely on per-host default workspaces — unify on one peer ID + one workspace or get N disconnected representations (OC v2 has already been bitten by stranded peers).
Do NOT call set_card with a partial list expecting a merge — it overwrites the entire 40-fact card.
Do NOT block the agent waiting for reasoning, or assert on chat()/semantic results immediately after a write — reasoning is async/eventually-consistent.
Do NOT enable observe_others everywhere or leave observe_me on for deterministic bots/sub-agents — wasted compute and needless complexity.
Do NOT scope sessions so thin they never reach the ~1,000-token batch threshold, or Honcho will appear to never learn.

topic: GBrain (Garry Tan's Postgres-native personal knowledge brain): retrieval/merge-layer design, self-wiring graph, synthesis layer, dream enrichment, and markdown-as-system-of-record — and what OpenComputer v2 should adopt for its triple-memory merge layer
key_patterns: FOUR-STRATEGY HYBRID RETRIEVAL FUSED BY RRF (the core merge layer). GBrain layers (1) vector = HNSW on pgvector over chunk embeddings, (2) BM25 keyword via Postgres tsvector, (3) Reciprocal Rank Fusion merging the two ranked lists, (4) typed-edge graph traversal as a recall arm. RRF is rank-based not score-based: score = sum over retrievers of 1/(rank+k); Azure AI Search and the broader literature converge on k=60 (small k favors top picks, larger k rewards cross-retriever consensus and dodges the score-normalization problem entirely — a BM25 score of 8.3 and a cosine of 0.9 are never compared directly, only their ranks). GBrain's measured payoff: graph + extract-quality lift gives P@5 49.1 / R@5 97.9 vs ~18 P@5 for vector-only, ripgrep-BM25-only, OR hybrid-without-graph — i.e. +31 P@5 points, the graph is the load-bearing wall, not the vector index. (docs/architecture/RETRIEVAL.md)

SOURCE-TIER BOOST AS A SQL CASE PRIOR, APPLIED IN THE OUTER RE-RANK. A source-factor CASE expression (src/core/search/sql-ranking.ts) multiplies scores by curation tier: curated dirs (originals/, concepts/, writing/) outrank bulk dirs (chat/, daily/, media/). Three tiers of behavior: hard-exclude prefixes (test/, attachments/, .raw/) filter at retrieval BEFORE ranking; demote (archive/ at 0.5x) is a soft prior the reranker can still override; promote is the boost. Config via env GBRAIN_SOURCE_BOOST or per-call exclude_slug_prefixes. Temporal/high-detail queries BYPASS the boost so fresh chat pages re-surface. Lesson: tier weighting is a multiplicative prior on the fused score, NOT a hard filter — keep a cross-encoder able to rescue a demoted-but-relevant page.

RERANKER RUNS AFTER RRF+GRAPH, ON A SMALL CANDIDATE WINDOW. Default is ZeroEntropy zerank-2 cross-encoder reranking the top ~30 fused candidates; it reshuffles 60% of top-1 results because hybrid ranking is locally optimal per strategy but globally suboptimal — a cross-encoder reads query+doc jointly with full attention and catches the case where vector+keyword+graph all agreed on a semantically-related-but-topically-wrong doc. Cost: +150ms p50, ~$0.025/M tokens, and it's invisible for agent loops that do downstream LLM work anyway. Matches Azure's model: semantic/reranker score is reported SEPARATELY (@search.rerankerScore 0-4) and runs AFTER RRF merging, never folded into the fusion score. Reranker is a config toggle (search.reranker.enabled) and has a fully-local llama.cpp cross-encoder recipe (Qwen3-Reranker) behind the same gateway.rerank() seam — model-agnostic by construction.

FULL PIPELINE AS REPLACEABLE STAGES: intent-classify (deterministic, no LLM; entity/temporal/event/general route to different ranking knobs) -> optional multi-query expansion (Haiku produces 2-3 variants, each runs full hybrid, merged via RRF; opt-in per cost tier) -> hybrid (vector + keyword + relational + source-aware CASE re-rank + RRF -> top 30) -> graph augment (typed-edge traversal from seed) -> cross-encoder rerank (30 -> reordered) -> token-budget enforcement -> dedup by slug (keep best chunk). Every stage is independently testable and replaceable; whole orchestration is <1ms, latency lives in HTTP calls (embed, rerank) and index scans. Named search MODES (conservative/balanced/tokenmax) bundle the cost/quality knobs (token budget, expansion on/off, reranker on/off, relational on/off) into ONE config key with a documented resolution chain.

ZERO-LLM SELF-WIRING KNOWLEDGE GRAPH. Every put_page runs extractEntityRefs over the markdown body with THREE regexes (markdown links [text](slug), Obsidian [[slug|alias]] wikilinks, typed-link blockquotes) and a single SQL addLinksBatch (INSERT...SELECT FROM jsonb_to_recordset JOIN pages ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING) — zero LLM tokens, full 17K-page graph extract in seconds. Edge TYPES (attended, works_at, invested_in, founded, advises, mentions) are inferred heuristically from surrounding sentence context, also LLM-free. This is the opposite of Microsoft GraphRAG's LLM-per-document entity/relation extraction ($20-500 indexing). The new entity becomes a page stub automatically (graph grows on every write). Multi-hop traversal via graph-query answers relationship questions vector search structurally cannot ('who works at Acme', 'what did Bob invest in this quarter').

PER-PAGE MAX-POOL + TITLE-PHRASE BOOST + ALIAS HOP + EVIDENCE/CREATE-SAFETY CONTRACT for named-thing retrieval. (1) Vector side collapses chunk-grain candidates to best chunk per page (DISTINCT ON slug) over the FULL candidate set before LIMIT, so a page surfaces on its strongest evidence instead of losing to a neighbor on one weak chunk. (2) Title-phrase boost: when the normalized query is a contiguous token-run inside page.title, a bounded multiplier fires. (3) Alias hop: free-text aliases: frontmatter is projected into a page_aliases table and consulted at query time — the only layer that bridges true synonyms with zero surface overlap. (4) Every result carries evidence (alias_hit | exact_title_match | high_vector_match | keyword_exact | weak_semantic) and create_safety (exists | probable | unknown) so an agent decides whether a page already exists from a labeled signal, NOT a raw blended score. Each result also gets graph signals (adjacency boost / cross-source corroboration / session demote) with --explain per-stage attribution.

SYNTHESIS / 'THINK' LAYER = RETRIEVE THEN COMPOSE A CITED ANSWER WITH EXPLICIT GAP ANALYSIS. search returns ranked pages (fast, no LLM); think runs the SAME retrieval then composes synthesized prose where every claim traces to a source page slug AND appends an honest note on what the brain doesn't know (stale page, uncited claim, two pages contradicting, a hole to fill). Iron rules from the query skill: every claim has a citation; gaps are flagged explicitly ('the brain doesn't have information on X') rather than hallucinated; conflicting sources are noted with BOTH citations, never silently picked. The gap analysis is the explicit differentiator — it tells the user/agent what to go ask about instead of trusting a confident-but-incomplete answer.

DREAM CYCLE = NIGHTLY MULTI-PHASE ENRICHMENT CRON that compounds the brain while idle. 8-phase pipeline (lint -> backlinks -> sync -> synthesize -> extract -> patterns -> embed -> orphans). Phases: entity sweep (new entity -> create+enrich page; thin page -> fill out; existing -> append timeline), citation repair (add [Source:...] where missing, fix dead tweet URLs), consolidate (the ONE-WAY bridge promoting hot per-turn facts -> cold attributed takes with dedup + temporal reasoning), synthesize (Haiku verdict cached in dream_verdicts filters routine sessions; Sonnet writes pattern pages only above a min-evidence bar), contradiction probe wired in. Cost discipline: cheap-model verdict gate before expensive synthesis, persistent verdict cache (re-run = no-op), cooldown_hours cap (~2 synth runs/day), per-step est_seconds/est_usd plan with a spend cap that aborts runaway loops.

MARKDOWN-AS-SYSTEM-OF-RECORD; POSTGRES IS A DERIVED CACHE. The git repo of markdown+frontmatter is canonical; the DB is a rebuildable index (gbrain rebuild --confirm-destructive then sync+extract regenerates everything). Three table categories: FS-canonical (takes/facts/links/timeline/tags stored in fenced markdown blocks between <!--- gbrain:NAME:begin/end ---> markers, each with a parser + a round-trip writer + a reconciler cycle phase), derived-from-FS (pages/chunks/embeddings, rebuilt by chunker+embedder), and DB-only-by-design named exceptions (credentials, job queue, audit, caches). Consequences: disaster recovery is one command (no DB backup needed), multi-machine sync IS git, privacy via gitignoring sensitive pages (db_only), cross-agent collaboration because the fence is the merge point not the DB. A CI gate (check-system-of-record.sh) fails any new direct write to a derived table outside its reconciler. New user-knowledge category MUST land FS-first with a byte-identical round-trip test.

EPISTEMIC SEPARATION: TAKES vs FACTS. Two storage layers never conflated. Facts = hot, single-user (brain owner's per-turn conversational knowledge: event/preference/commitment/belief), captured real-time by a Haiku hook. Takes = cold, multi-holder (WHO believes WHAT with confidence weight + time: take/fact/bet/hunch attributed to any speaker), extracted from pages. The dream consolidate phase is the only one-way bridge facts->takes. Extraction-prompt lessons from a 100K-page run: holder != subject; atomic claims (split compounds); amplification != endorsement (retweet caps weight at 0.55); self-reported != verified (cap weight ~0.75, holder=person not world); no false precision (0.05 increments); a 'so what' signal-density filter.
tradeoffs: RRF (rank-based) vs weighted-score fusion: RRF needs no score normalization and is robust across retrievers with incomparable score ranges (BM25 unbounded, cosine 0.33-1.0), but it DISCARDS score magnitude — a barely-ranked-#1 and a dominant-#1 contribute identically. Azure's answer is optional per-retriever WEIGHT MULTIPLIERS applied to each retriever's contribution before summing (default 1.0; 0.5 demotes a vector arm, 2.0 promotes it) — a middle path GBrain mirrors with its per-mode relational/source knobs. k tuning is the lever: small k (~10-20) trusts top picks, k=60 rewards consensus; the right value is corpus-dependent.

Cross-encoder reranking buys large precision (60% top-1 reshuffle, +15-25% on benchmarks per the hybrid literature) at +150ms latency and per-query token cost, and it requires a hosted reranker API (or a self-hosted cross-encoder). It's worth it when a downstream LLM consumes the results (latency hidden) and NOT worth always-on for cheap high-volume paths — hence GBrain gates it behind cost tiers (off in conservative, on in balanced/tokenmax).

Self-wiring zero-LLM graph (regex entity-ref extraction + heuristic edge types) is nearly free and rebuilds in seconds, but it only captures relationships you explicitly wrote as links/wikilinks/typed blockquotes plus shallow sentence-context type inference. It will NOT discover implicit relations buried in prose the way an LLM extraction pass (GraphRAG) would. GBrain accepts thinner edges in exchange for near-zero cost and instant rebuild; GraphRAG accepts $20-500 indexing + super-linear index growth + 2-3x query latency for richer, LLM-inferred relations and community summaries.

Markdown-as-source-of-record gives free git versioning, one-command disaster recovery, and human-editable knowledge, but forces a strict discipline: every derived table needs a parser + round-trip writer + reconciler + byte-identical test, and a CI gate to stop direct DB writes from drifting. The DB can never be authoritative for user knowledge — every new knowledge type pays the FS-first tax. The payoff (rebuildable, portable, mergeable) only holds if that discipline is enforced mechanically.

Source-tier boost as a multiplicative SQL prior is cheap and tunable but encodes a static curation hierarchy that can be wrong for a given query; GBrain mitigates with query-intent bypass (temporal queries skip the boost) and by making the demote soft (reranker can rescue). Hard-exclude prefixes are faster but unrecoverable — only safe for true non-content (test/, attachments/, .raw/).

Named search modes (conservative/balanced/tokenmax) trade a 25x cost spread for one config dial. The convenience hides a real failure mode: a MISMATCH between mode and downstream model wastes capacity (tokenmax+Haiku overwhelms a cheap model; conservative+Opus starves an expensive one). The single knob is only good if the resolution chain and the cost matrix are documented so operators pair mode to model deliberately.
failure_modes: Cache-key contamination across heterogeneous configs: a query-result cache keyed only on the query text will serve a tokenmax (expansion-on, limit-50) result to a conservative read, or an OpenAI-1536d-embedding row to a Voyage-1024d query. GBrain's fix is folding a knobs_hash (mode knobs + active embedding column + provider + relational flag) into the cache key — versioned (KNOBS_HASH_VERSION) so each change forces a one-time miss spike instead of silent wrong answers. ANY merge-layer cache must key on the full retrieval config, not just the query.

Vector chunk-vs-page collapse bug: returning N chunks that downstream collapse to fewer pages silently under-delivers distinct results; a page can lose to a neighbor on one weak chunk. Fix: max-pool to best-chunk-per-page (DISTINCT ON slug) over the full candidate set BEFORE applying the user LIMIT.

True-synonym / named-thing misses: pure embedding proximity fails on chosen names with zero surface overlap ('Hall of Light' -> the Mingtang page). Vector+BM25 both miss it. Requires an explicit alias table consulted at query time; without it, retrieval silently returns nothing and an agent may create a DUPLICATE page (why create_safety exists/probable/unknown labels matter).

JSONB double-encoding: JSON.stringify into a ::jsonb cast double-encodes under postgres.js while PGLite hides the bug — a portability landmine. Pass raw objects to the engine; guard with a CI grep. (Relevant to any Postgres-backed memory store that runs on more than one driver.)

Auto-supersession over-eagerness: a contradiction/temporal probe that AUTO-applies valid_until writebacks will silently rewrite history on legitimate change-over-time. GBrain's invariant is 'NEVER auto-applies' — the probe only EMITS paste-ready resolution commands; a single explicit phase writes valid_until, pinned by a grep guard. Temporal change must be distinguished from contradiction (six-member verdict enum: no_contradiction | contradiction | temporal_supersession | temporal_regression | temporal_evolution | negation_artifact) or the probe cries wolf on every metric that legitimately moved.

Synthesis/enrichment cron burning money or going rogue: an unbounded nightly synthesis loop can rack up LLM spend or (under prompt injection) write to arbitrary paths. Guards: a cheap-model verdict GATE before expensive synthesis, a persistent verdict cache (idempotent re-runs), a per-step est_usd spend cap that aborts the plan, a cooldown_hours throttle, and a server-enforced allowed_slug_prefixes trust boundary so even a compromised subagent can only write to whitelisted dirs.

tsvector dictionary mismatch on multilingual corpora: using the 'english' dictionary silently drops recall on non-English content (stemming/stop-words wrong). A keyword arm must pick the right FTS config per language or it degrades invisibly.
adopt: Build v2's triple-memory MERGE layer on rank-based Reciprocal Rank Fusion, not weighted score blending. For each of the three planes (Honcho semantic, GBrain graph, local markdown), take each backend's ranked list and fuse with score = sum 1/(rank+k), k=60 default and tunable. This sidesteps the impossible problem of normalizing Honcho relevance vs GBrain hybrid score vs local BM25 onto one scale — exactly the problem the current aggregator faces. Keep an optional per-PLANE weight multiplier (default 1.0) so a plane can be promoted/demoted per query-intent (Azure's model).

Add a SOURCE-TIER / plane-tier prior as a multiplicative factor on the fused score, applied as an outer re-rank, not a hard filter: e.g. user-authored local markdown and curated GBrain pages outrank bulk auto-captured chat/honcho rows; demote (0.5x) stale/archive content rather than hiding it; reserve hard-exclude only for true non-content. Make it a config map (env/per-call) and bypass it for temporal queries so fresh context re-surfaces.

Put a cross-encoder RERANK pass AFTER fusion, on the top ~20-30 merged candidates only, gated behind a cost tier (off for cheap/high-volume, on for the synthesis path where a downstream LLM hides the latency). Use a provider-agnostic rerank() seam with a fallback to a local llama.cpp cross-encoder so it never hardcodes a vendor (aligns with v2's model-agnostic standing rule). Report the reranker score SEPARATELY from the fusion score for explainability.

Adopt the search vs think split as two distinct merge-layer surfaces: a fast raw-retrieval call (fused+reranked pages, no LLM) for agent context windows, and a synthesis call that composes a CITED answer with explicit GAP ANALYSIS (every claim -> source; flag stale/uncited/contradicting/missing). v2's memory tab already aggregates three planes — give it a 'think' mode that emits citations + a 'what the brain doesn't know' note instead of a raw list. This is the single highest-leverage GBrain idea to port.

Treat local markdown as the SYSTEM OF RECORD for user-authored memory and the DBs (Honcho/GBrain/SQLite) as rebuildable derived caches. Concretely: write durable facts/takes/timeline into fenced markdown blocks (<!--- begin/end --->) with a parser + round-trip writer + a reconciler that rebuilds the index, and a CI/test guard that disaster recovery (wipe derived store -> re-sync from markdown) reproduces counts. This gives v2 free git versioning, portability across the Hetzner VM / local / frontend, and one-command recovery — and fits v2's edge-not-core idiom (a sync skill + reconciler, not new core schema).

Add a zero-LLM self-wiring link/graph pass on every memory write: regex-extract [[wiki]] / [text](slug) refs from markdown, upsert edges with ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING in one batched SQL call, auto-stub new entities. It's near-free, rebuilds in seconds, and turns the local markdown plane into a graph the merge layer can traverse for relationship queries — the +31 P@5 lever — without paying GraphRAG's LLM-extraction cost.

Borrow the cost-discipline harness for any v2 dreaming/enrichment cron: cheap-model verdict gate before expensive synthesis, persistent idempotent verdict cache, est_usd spend cap that aborts, cooldown throttle, and a server-enforced allowed-write-prefix trust boundary for any autonomous subagent. v2 already has a dreaming plugin and self-evolution loop — wire these guards in so it compounds safely while idle.

Key any merge-layer result CACHE on the full retrieval config (query + plane weights + embedding model/provider + mode knobs), versioned so a knob change forces a clean miss instead of serving a stale-config answer. Add evidence + create_safety labels (alias_hit/title_match/high_vector/keyword/weak + exists/probable/unknown) to results so the agent decides dedup/write from a labeled signal, not a raw score.
avoid: Do NOT blend the three planes by normalizing their raw scores onto one scale and weighting — score ranges are incomparable (Honcho vs hybrid vs BM25) and any fixed normalization is brittle. Use rank-based RRF instead.

Do NOT make any v2 memory backend authoritative for user knowledge if you adopt markdown-as-source-of-record — putting durable knowledge DB-only breaks the rebuild guarantee. Either it's FS-canonical (with a reconciler) or it's an explicit DB-only-by-design exception (caches, credentials, job state). No 'DB-only for now.'

Do NOT add an LLM-per-document GraphRAG-style entity/relation extraction pass for the graph plane: it costs $20-500 to index a real corpus, grows super-linearly, adds 2-3x query latency, and re-indexing on every change is infeasible for a live brain. The zero-LLM regex+heuristic graph captures the high-value explicit relations at ~free. (Adopt LLM extraction only as an opt-in enrichment for a curated subset, never the default path.)

Do NOT let any contradiction/temporal probe AUTO-apply edits (valid_until, supersession) — it will silently rewrite legitimate change-over-time. Emit paste-ready resolution commands; gate writes behind one explicit phase. And distinguish temporal evolution from real contradiction or the probe cries wolf constantly.

Do NOT always-on the cross-encoder reranker or LLM query-expansion on cheap/high-volume paths — the per-query latency + token cost is real money at scale. Gate them behind cost tiers; default the high-volume path to vector+keyword+RRF+graph with no LLM in the loop.
Do NOT cache merged results on the query string alone — it serves wrong-config answers across embedding-model/mode changes. Always fold the retrieval config into the cache key.

Do NOT hard-exclude content you might later want (archive/, older chat) — demote it as a soft prior so the reranker can rescue a relevant page; reserve hard-exclude for true non-content (test fixtures, attachments, raw sidecars).

topic: Retrieval and ranking across multiple heterogeneous stores (FTS5 keyword + graph brain + identity/peer provider) — production merge-layer patterns and a concrete design for OpenComputer v2
key_patterns: PARALLEL FAN-OUT THEN FUSE (the baseline hybrid pattern). Fire one query at every store concurrently, collect each store's own ranked list, then combine into a single ranked list. This is what Elastic RRF retriever, OpenSearch normalization-processor, LangChain EnsembleRetriever and LlamaIndex QueryFusionRetriever all do. Each store keeps its native scoring (BM25 for FTS5, cosine for vectors, graph relevance for the brain); fusion happens only at the merge step so the stores stay decoupled. This is the correct DEFAULT for v2 because the three stores answer overlapping-but-different questions and you rarely know in advance which holds the answer.

RECIPROCAL RANK FUSION (RRF) — the rank-only fusion that needs no calibration. Formula (Elastic/LangChain, default k=60): score(d) = sum over stores of 1/(k + rank_store(d)), rank starting at 1; a doc absent from a store contributes 0 for that store. It ignores raw scores entirely and uses only position, so it is immune to incompatible score scales (BM25 0..30 vs cosine 0..1 vs a graph-walk score) and to outliers. Docs appearing in multiple stores get additive boosts (consensus reward). k controls how much rank depth matters: higher k lets lower-ranked docs influence more; k=60 is the universal default across Elastic, LangChain, and the original Cormack 2009 paper. This is the single highest-leverage primitive for v2's merge layer.

WEIGHTED SCORE FUSION (normalize-then-linear-combine) — used when you trust scores and want per-store control. OpenSearch normalization-processor: normalize each store's scores (min_max or L2; z-score in 3.0+) onto a common range, then combine via weighted arithmetic/geometric/harmonic mean with an explicit weights array (their example: 0.3/0.7). Anthropic Contextual Retrieval uses exactly this shape: dense weight 1.0, BM25 sparse weight 0.25. Linear combination can slightly beat RRF on relevance metrics WHEN you have a reliable normalizer and tuned weights — but it requires that tuning, which RRF does not.

LEARNED / CROSS-ENCODER RERANK as a final cascade stage (two-stage retrieval). Stage 1: cheap recall — fan out + RRF to gather a wide candidate pool (Anthropic used top-150; Pinecone example top-25). Stage 2: a cross-encoder reranker (bge-reranker-v2-m3, Cohere Rerank v3.5, or FlashRank) scores each (query, candidate) jointly and keeps top-K (Anthropic top-20; Pinecone top-3). Rationale from Pinecone: retrieve wide to maximize recall, then prune hard to maximize the LLM's recall (LLM accuracy degrades as you stuff context). Cross-encoders are accurate because they read query+doc together, but they CANNOT be precomputed (must run at query time) — that's the whole cost. Anthropic's measured impact: contextual-embeddings alone cut top-20 failures 35%, +BM25 fusion 49%, +reranking 67%.

QUERY ROUTING / CLASSIFICATION vs ALWAYS-PARALLEL. A router (LLM tool-call, semantic classifier, or rules) decides up front whether to retrieve at all and which store(s) to hit, instead of always fanning out to all three. Routing decisions in the literature: (a) retrieval-necessity (skip retrieval if the model already knows — retrieval adds latency), (b) retriever selection (sparse/FTS5 excels at exact entity/keyword match; dense/graph at semantic/relational), (c) source selection (hit one store or a combination). The tradeoff: routing saves latency and cost and reduces cross-store noise, but a wrong route silently drops the store that actually had the answer. Track wrong-route rate as a first-class metric.

LAYERED / CASCADE LOOKUP (cheap-first, escalate). Try the cheapest store first (FTS5 keyword — sub-ms, local), and only escalate to vector/graph/network calls if the cheap layer's top result is weak (low confidence or empty). Microsoft DRIFT search is the graph instance of this: start with a light global pass over community summaries, then expand only promising branches into local entity-neighborhood search, terminating after a bounded number of iterations. Cascade minimizes average latency and cost on the common case where the cheap layer suffices; the failure mode is under-escalation (cheap layer looks confident but is wrong, so you never consult the store that knew).

HETEROGENEOUS-RESULT UNIFICATION — normalize every store's hit to one common 'candidate' record before fusing. GraphRAG/DRIFT does this by embedding entity descriptions, relationship descriptions, AND community summaries as vectors so a graph node and a text chunk live in one comparable space. The portable pattern: map each store's native object (a Honcho peer-representation string, an FTS5 message row, a GBrain graph node/triple) to {id, text_for_rerank, source_store, native_rank, native_score, metadata}. Fusion (RRF) operates only on id + native_rank, so it is type-agnostic by construction; the reranker operates on text_for_rerank, so each heterogeneous type just needs a canonical text serialization (a triple becomes 'subject — relation — object'; a peer rep is already a string).

LATENCY BUDGETING WITH PER-STORE TIMEOUTS + PARTIAL RESULTS. In any fan-out, the overall p99 approaches the SLOWEST leaf (a remote Honcho/GBrain HTTP call), not the average. Production fixes: (1) a hard per-store timeout set from that store's measured p99 — fail fast, never let one store drag the turn; (2) PARTIAL RESULTS — fuse whatever returned by the deadline and drop laggards (RRF degrades gracefully because a missing store just contributes 0); (3) HEDGED REQUESTS for replicated/cacheable stores — send a backup in-flight and take the first responder (InfoQ reports ~74% p99 reduction with adaptive hedging). The local FTS5 layer should be the synchronous backbone; remote semantic stores are best-effort within the budget.
tradeoffs: RRF vs weighted score fusion: RRF needs zero tuning and is robust to incompatible score scales and outliers (ignores magnitudes) — but it THROWS AWAY score magnitude, so a store that returns 10 near-identical-quality hits is treated the same as one with a single sharp winner. Weighted fusion preserves magnitude and can slightly beat RRF on relevance WHEN you have a trustworthy per-store normalizer and tuned weights — but min_max normalization is outlier-sensitive (one huge score compresses everyone else) and degenerates when a store returns a single result (min==max). For v2's mismatched stores, RRF is the safer default; offer weighted fusion only once you can calibrate.

Routing vs always-parallel: routing cuts latency/cost and removes irrelevant-store noise, but every wrong route is a silent recall miss with no fallback. Always-parallel maximizes recall and never misses the right store, at the cost of always paying the slowest store's latency and the risk of one store's noise polluting the fused list. Hybrid sweet spot: always hit the cheap local store, route the EXPENSIVE remote stores.

Cross-encoder rerank vs fusion-only: rerank gives the biggest single relevance jump (Anthropic: 49%->67% failure reduction) and lets you retrieve wide then prune hard — but it adds real latency you cannot precompute (CPU MiniLM on ~50 candidates ~100-250ms; FlashRank ~15-30ms; Cohere API ~150-400ms + network) and a model/API dependency. Fusion-only is near-free and local but tops out lower on relevance.

Cascade vs parallel: cascade minimizes average latency/cost (cheap layer answers the common case) but adds serial round-trips on hard queries (worse worst-case latency) and risks under-escalation. Parallel pays full latency every time but has a tight, predictable bound and never under-consults.

Consensus boost (RRF rewards docs in multiple lists) is usually good (agreement = confidence) but it can DEMOTE a uniquely-correct answer that only one store could possibly know — e.g. an identity fact only the peer/Honcho store holds will never get a multi-store boost and can be outranked by a mediocre fact that appears in two stores. Mitigation: per-source floors or a source-aware weight so a sole-source identity hit isn't buried.

Tight per-store timeouts protect p99 but can systematically starve a legitimately slower-but-richer store (the graph brain) on every turn, quietly making it dead weight. Budget must be set from real measured p99, not a guess, and monitored for 'store X never makes the deadline' regressions.
failure_modes: Score-scale collision: naively concatenating or numerically comparing BM25 scores (0..~30, unbounded) with cosine (0..1) and a graph-walk score makes one store always dominate. This is the classic reason to use RRF (rank-only) or mandatory per-store normalization before any linear combine.

min_max normalization blowups: a single outlier score compresses all other normalized scores toward 0; and when a store returns exactly one result, min==max gives a divide-by-zero / undefined normalized score. Guard both cases or prefer RRF.

Silent recall loss from routing: a misclassified query is routed away from the only store with the answer and there is no signal that anything was missed — the agent just confidently answers wrong. Needs wrong-route monitoring and a parallel-fallback escape hatch.

Tail-latency amplification in fan-out: overall turn latency tracks the slowest store, so one wedged remote provider (Honcho/GBrain network hiccup) blows the whole per-turn recall budget unless there is a hard timeout + partial-result fusion. v2's current concatenate-providers path has no deadline, so a hung provider can stall the turn.

Unique-answer demotion under consensus fusion: an identity/peer fact that by definition lives in only one store gets no multi-store boost and can be outranked by a less-relevant fact present in two stores. Per-source floor or source weighting required.

Reranking a polluted candidate pool: if stage-1 recall is bad, a strong reranker just confidently reorders garbage — rerank improves PRECISION over the candidate set, it cannot recover recall the fan-out failed to fetch. Get recall right first.

Heterogeneous serialization loss: feeding a raw graph triple or a raw peer-representation blob to a reranker without a clean canonical text form gives the cross-encoder noise, degrading its scores; each store type needs a deliberate text_for_rerank projection.

Under-escalation in cascade: the cheap keyword layer returns a plausible-looking but wrong top hit with apparent high confidence, so the system never escalates to the store that actually knew — calibrate the escalation threshold on real misses, don't trust raw top-1 score.
adopt: Build a thin MergeLayer that sits ABOVE the existing providers (FTS5 session-search, Honcho peer, GBrain graph) and does fan-out + fuse + optional rerank — DO NOT touch the stores. This fits v2's 'capability at the edges' idiom: it is a new step in MemoryManager.build context, not a new core tool. Today MemoryManager just concatenates each provider's pre-formatted text block in order (builtin first, one external) — replace that concat with: gather ranked candidates from each provider, fuse, then render ONE ranked block.

Make each provider return a list of CANDIDATES, not a pre-baked text block: {id, text_for_rerank, source_store, native_rank, native_score?, metadata}. Add a small adapter per store (FTS5 row -> text; Honcho peer-rep string -> text; GBrain node/triple -> 'subj — rel — obj'). This is the heterogeneous-unification step and it is the prerequisite for any real fusion.

Default fusion = RRF with k=60 (Elastic/LangChain/Cormack default). It is one function, needs zero calibration, and is the only fusion that is safe given v2's three wildly different score scales. Add a per-source weight multiplier on each store's RRF contribution (weighted RRF, as LangChain's EnsembleRetriever does) so you can later up-weight identity/Honcho for personal-fact queries — default all weights to 1.0.

Adopt two-stage retrieval: fan-out+RRF to a WIDE pool (~50-150 candidates), then rerank to a SMALL set (~10-20) for the prompt. Gate the reranker behind config/service-availability (model-agnostic: bge-reranker-v2-m3 local, or Cohere/an OpenRouter reranker if configured), default OFF so there is zero new hard dependency and zero latency cost until a user opts in. This mirrors Anthropic's 150->20 and Pinecone's 25->3.

Treat the LOCAL FTS5 store as the synchronous backbone and the REMOTE semantic stores (Honcho, GBrain) as best-effort within a latency budget. Give each remote store a hard per-store timeout derived from its measured p99; on deadline, fuse partial results (RRF degrades gracefully — a missing store contributes 0). This directly removes v2's current 'a hung provider stalls the turn' risk.

Add per-source floors / source-aware weighting so a sole-source identity fact (only Honcho/peer store can hold it) is not buried by consensus boosting. Simplest: guarantee top-N slots reserved for the identity provider when the query looks identity/personal, OR bump its weight via the query router.

Start with always-parallel for the local layer and a CHEAP rules/LLM-tool router only for the expensive remote stores (route, don't always fan out the network calls). Keep an always-parallel fallback so a misroute can't silently zero out recall. Log wrong-route rate.

Extend the existing recall_probe.py (memory-stack/recall_probe.py already measures precision@1, recall@5, MRR over FTS5) into a CROSS-STORE eval that seeds facts into all three stores and measures fused recall@5 — make it the regression gate for the merge layer, exactly as it already gates FTS5 with RECALL_FLOOR=0.8. Without this you cannot tell if fusion helped or hurt.
avoid: Do NOT numerically compare or sum raw cross-store scores (BM25 vs cosine vs graph-walk) without normalization — it lets one store dominate by scale accident. Either RRF (rank-only) or normalize-first.

Do NOT default to min_max-normalized weighted fusion before you have tuned weights and a robust normalizer — it is outlier-sensitive and breaks on single-result stores. RRF first; weighted fusion is an opt-in upgrade once you can calibrate.

Do NOT make the reranker or any remote semantic store a hard dependency of the per-turn recall path — gate them behind config + availability, keep local FTS5 as the always-works floor (consistent with the model-agnostic and no-placeholder standing rules).
Do NOT add an unbounded fan-out with no per-store timeout — that re-introduces the tail-latency stall. Every remote store needs a deadline and partial-result fusion.
Do NOT route the cheap LOCAL store away — only route the expensive remote stores, and always keep a parallel fallback so a misclassification can't silently drop the store that had the answer.
Do NOT rely on reranking to fix bad recall — it reorders the candidate pool, it cannot fetch what stage-1 missed. Fix fan-out recall first, then rerank for precision.
Do NOT bake a single vendor's reranker (Cohere) in as the only option — keep it pluggable (local bge-reranker / FlashRank / API) to honor v2's model-agnostic rule.

Do NOT replace v2's existing per-provider injection-threat scan / sanitize_context fence when adding the merge layer — keep fusing AFTER sanitization so untrusted store output can't smuggle prompt-injection into the ranked block.

topic: Hybrid agent memory architectures in production (2026): how the strongest teams (Mem0, Letta/MemGPT, Zep/Graphiti, Cognee, LangMem, LlamaIndex, Supermemory, Anthropic) build write/recall/update/scope, benchmarked against OpenComputer v2
key_patterns: LLM-as-memory-controller for the WRITE path (extract-then-reconcile), not blind append. Mem0's canonical two-phase pipeline: (1) EXTRACTION calls an LLM over a rolling conversation summary S + a window of the last ~10 messages + the new message pair to emit a small set of 'salient' candidate facts; (2) UPDATE retrieves the top-s (~10) semantically-similar existing memories and lets the LLM choose ADD / UPDATE / DELETE / NOOP per candidate via a tool-call. The LLM IS the state-transition controller; there is no separate classifier. This is the single most-copied pattern in the field and what turns raw transcripts into a deduplicated, contradiction-resolved store.

Two-tier memory split: always-in-context curated blocks vs out-of-context retrieved store. Letta/MemGPT is the archetype: 'core memory' = small labeled, editable, persistent strings (block has label + description + value + char-limit) that live permanently in the prompt (RAM), edited by the agent itself via tools (memory_replace/memory_insert/memory_rethink, a.k.a. core_memory_append/replace); 'archival memory' = external vector store queried explicitly via archival_memory_search; 'recall memory' = searchable conversation history. The agent self-curates what is promoted into the small always-on tier. LlamaIndex generalizes this as composable Memory blocks (Static/FactExtraction/Vector) with per-block priority for token-budget eviction.

Temporal knowledge graph with bi-temporal validity and edge invalidation INSTEAD of deletion (Zep/Graphiti). Three subgraphs: episodic (raw, non-lossy), semantic entity (extracted entities + labeled fact-edges), community (clustered summaries). Every fact-edge carries four timestamps: t_created / t_expired (transaction time: when the system learned/retired it) and t_valid / t_invalid (event time: when the fact was actually true). On a new contradicting fact, the LLM compares it against semantically-related existing edges and INVALIDATES the old edge (sets t_invalid = t_valid of the new edge) rather than deleting it - history is preserved and you can reason 'as of' any point in time. New information is consistently prioritized.

Hybrid recall = semantic + lexical + graph, then rerank. The leaders never rely on cosine-similarity alone. Zep composes cosine + Okapi BM25 (Lucene) + breadth-first n-hop graph traversal, then reranks with RRF / MMR / node-distance / cross-encoder. Anthropic's Contextual Retrieval pairs contextual embeddings + contextual BM25 + a reranker (failure rate 5.7%->3.7% with contextual embeddings, ->2.9% adding BM25, ->1.9% adding rerank). Cognee/Mem0g/Supermemory all back a vector store with a graph for relationship traversal. The merge-and-rerank step across stores is a first-class component, not an afterthought.

Asynchronous / background ('sleep-time' or 'subconscious') memory formation off the hot path. LangMem's explicit dichotomy: hot-path tools the agent calls mid-conversation (immediate, but adds user-visible latency) vs a background memory manager that reflects and extracts after the turn. Letta calls these sleep-time agents; Mem0 makes addMemories async; Mem0 also runs an asynchronous summary-refresh module. The pattern: keep the response turn fast, do expensive extraction/consolidation in the background.

Externalize memory to a file/tool the agent operates, and aggressively prune the live context (Anthropic). The memory tool is a client-side, developer-owned file system in a /memories dir (create/read/str_replace/insert/delete/rename) that persists across sessions. Pair it with context editing (auto-clear of stale tool calls/results near the token limit). Reported: context editing alone +29%, memory tool + context editing +39% over baseline; a 100-turn web-search eval cut tokens 84% and completed tasks that otherwise failed. The governing principle is 'the smallest set of high-signal tokens' to fight context rot (recall degrades as the window fills).

Multi-scope identity tags composed at retrieval, with shared blocks for cross-agent state. Mem0 tags every write with user_id / agent_id / run_id (session) / app_id|org_id and composes/merges/ranks across scopes at read time, giving both per-agent isolation and shared org memory from one store. Letta shares the SAME memory block object across multiple agents (one writes, all see it instantly) with read_only protection for blocks an agent must not mutate. LangMem scopes via hierarchical LangGraph store namespaces (org/user/app) templated from runtime config.

Memory typing: semantic (facts) vs episodic (past interactions/few-shot successes) vs procedural (behavior rules / evolving system prompt). LangMem is the clearest taxonomy and uniquely treats procedural memory as a self-improving prompt via create_prompt_optimizer (analyzes trajectories + feedback, rewrites instructions). Episodic memory stores not just the outcome but the situation + reasoning + why it worked, replayed as few-shot examples.
tradeoffs: Extraction (LLM-distilled facts) vs raw storage: extraction gives ~93% token reduction at recall and a clean store, but every write costs LLM calls (Cognee: 2 LLM calls per chunk; Mem0: extract + per-candidate update calls) and silently drops nuance. Mem0's own numbers show the quality cost: 66.9% LLM-judge vs 72.9% for naive full-context on LOCOMO - extraction trades a few points of accuracy for 90%+ latency/token wins. Zep mitigates by keeping a non-lossy episodic layer underneath the extracted graph so nothing is truly lost.

Knowledge graph (Zep/Graphiti, Cognee, Mem0g) vs flat vector store (vanilla Mem0, classic RAG): graphs enable multi-hop reasoning, entity resolution, and clean bi-temporal contradiction handling, and Zep shows the biggest LongMemEval gains (+15-18% over full-context) precisely on enterprise-scale (115k-token) histories. But graphs add an entity-resolution/dedup step, more LLM calls, write amplification, and operational complexity (a graph DB + vector DB + sometimes relational, all kept in sync). For short histories (DMR, ~60 msgs) the graph barely beats full-context - the win only materializes at long-horizon scale.

Always-in-context core blocks (Letta) vs pure retrieval: core blocks are zero-latency and never 'miss' (no retrieval failure), but they consume prompt budget on every call and their quality depends entirely on the model's self-editing judgment - a weak model curates a weak core. Retrieval stores scale infinitely but can miss the relevant memory. The mature answer is both tiers, with a promotion policy deciding what earns a slot in the small always-on tier.

Hot-path vs background extraction: hot-path memory is immediately available next turn but adds visible latency and can derail the agent's task focus; background/sleep-time extraction keeps turns fast but introduces a consistency lag (a fact mentioned this turn may not be retrievable until the background job runs). Most production systems run background by default and reserve hot-path writes for explicit 'remember this' moments.

Profiles (single structured doc, overwrite-in-place) vs collections (unbounded append-and-search): LangMem frames the choice - profiles stay bounded and are trivially looked up but can only hold 'latest state' and lose history; collections capture everything but force you to solve consolidation, contradiction, and unbounded growth. Choosing per memory-type (profile for user preferences, collection for events) avoids forcing one model everywhere.

Invalidate-don't-delete (Zep t_invalid, Mem0 'mark invalid not physical remove') preserves auditability and time-travel reasoning but grows the store monotonically and adds filter cost on every read (must exclude expired edges). True forgetting/decay (Supermemory's relevance decay + recency bias) bounds growth and mirrors human memory but risks dropping a fact that becomes relevant later. The field is split; safest is soft-invalidate + a separate decay/archival sweep.
failure_modes: Over-extraction / write amplification: running an LLM extractor on every message pair (plus per-candidate ADD/UPDATE/DELETE decisions, plus graph entity-resolution, plus summarization) explodes cost and latency and produces noisy near-duplicate memories. Cognee's 2-LLM-calls-per-chunk and Mem0's per-candidate update calls show how quickly this scales; without batching/async it dominates the bill.

Unbounded growth: invalidate-don't-delete + collection-style append with no decay, archival, or community-summarization sweep makes the store grow forever, slowing retrieval and raising rerank cost. Systems that only soft-invalidate (Zep, Mem0g) must pair it with Supermemory-style decay or Zep-style community consolidation or they bloat.

Context rot from over-stuffing the live window: pre-loading large retrieved blobs or never clearing stale tool results degrades recall as the window fills (Anthropic: accuracy drops as token count rises). The naive 'retrieve top-10 and stuff into every prompt' is exactly the anti-pattern Supermemory and Anthropic both call out.

Memory quality bottlenecked by model judgment: Letta-style self-editing and Mem0-style LLM-controller writes are only as good as the model doing them - a cheap/weak model produces a polluted store (wrong ADD vs UPDATE choices, bad entity resolution, hallucinated facts). The write path needs a capable model even if recall uses a cheap one.

Silent contradiction / stale facts when conflict detection is similarity-only: if 'I live in SF' and a later 'I moved to NYC' don't surface as semantically-related candidates, both persist and retrieval returns contradictory facts. Zep's explicit LLM contradiction check over semantically-related edges exists precisely to catch this; skipping it yields a store that confidently contradicts itself.

Cross-store retrieval that doesn't actually merge/rerank: bolting a vector store + graph + profile together but returning each independently (or only one) loses the complementary-signal benefit and can return duplicates or rank a weak vector hit above a strong graph fact. The merge+rerank stage is load-bearing and is the part teams most often under-build.

Scope leakage: forgetting to tag writes with user_id/agent_id/session and to filter on read leaks one user's or one agent's memory into another's context - both a correctness and a privacy/security failure. (v2 already saw a session-leak class bug; memory scoping is the same hazard one layer deeper.)
adopt: Adopt Mem0's two-phase WRITE policy as the v2 memory-write contract, implemented at the edge (in agent/memory_manager.py and the per-provider plugins, not as a new core tool): EXTRACT salient candidates from (rolling summary + recent window + new turn), then for each candidate retrieve top-k similar existing memories and have an LLM emit ADD/UPDATE/DELETE/NOOP. Use a capable model for the write path even if recall uses Haiku. This is the highest-leverage gap - v2's plugins (mem0, honcho, supermemory) provide substrates but the reconcile-on-write policy is what makes them not bloat.

Adopt a two-tier split natively: a small always-in-context 'core blocks' tier (label+description+value+char-limit, Letta-style, self-edited via a memory tool) layered on top of the existing retrieved providers. v2 already ships tools/memory_tool.py and the Anthropic memory tool spec - wire core blocks to it and let the agent promote durable facts (user identity, project state, standing preferences) into the always-on tier so they never depend on a retrieval hit.

Adopt invalidate-don't-delete with bi-temporal validity for any fact that can change (location, job, preferences). v2 already has agent/memory_versioning.py - extend it to store t_valid/t_invalid (event time) + t_created/t_expired (transaction time) and resolve contradictions by invalidating the superseded record, mirroring Zep. This gives correct 'as of' reasoning and an audit trail without a full graph DB.

Adopt hybrid recall + a real merge/rerank stage in the gateway/platforms/memory_aggregator.py: combine vector (semantic) + BM25/keyword (lexical) + the existing GBrain graph traversal, then RRF or cross-encoder rerank across ALL providers (honcho + mem0 + local + GBrain) into one ranked, deduplicated context block. This is Anthropic's contextual-retrieval result (failures 5.7%->1.9%) and Zep's φ composition - and it's the natural job of v2's aggregator, which today merges but should rerank.

Adopt background ('sleep-time') extraction as the default and keep the hot path fast: run extraction/consolidation in v2's already-existing dreaming plugin / async path, not inline. Reserve hot-path writes for explicit user 'remember this'. This matches LangMem/Letta/Mem0 and fits v2's dreaming + self-evolution loop already in the tree.

Adopt Anthropic context-editing in the live loop: auto-clear stale tool results near the token threshold and externalize durable state to the memory tool/files. v2 already has Hermes-native compaction; add tool-result clearing as the lightest-touch form (Anthropic: +29% alone, 84% token cut on 100-turn tasks). Pair with the 'smallest set of high-signal tokens' principle when building the injected memory block.

Adopt explicit multi-scope tagging (user_id / agent_id / session run_id / org app_id) on every write and scope-filtering on every read, composed at retrieval - directly applicable to v2's per-agent memory scope work (the explore-agents personas each having their own memory). Use Letta-style shared read_only blocks for org/team facts. This bounds the scope-leak failure mode v2 is already exposed to.

Adopt LangMem's memory typing where it pays: keep semantic facts in the reconciled store, store episodic successes (situation + reasoning + why it worked) as few-shot examples for the agent, and treat procedural memory as an optimizable system-prompt overlay - which v2's playbook-synthesis / self-evolution loop already gestures at. Reuse, don't rebuild.
avoid: Avoid blind append / raw-transcript dumping into the store. Every write must pass through extract+reconcile (ADD/UPDATE/DELETE/NOOP) or the store fills with noise and near-duplicates. This is the over-extraction-without-dedup failure that kills recall quality.

Avoid running the heavy extractor on every single message synchronously. Batch and/or push it to the background; per-message LLM extraction + graph resolution + summarization (Cognee's 2 calls/chunk x every chunk) is the write-amplification trap. Gate extraction frequency and run it off the hot path.

Avoid a memory store with no forgetting/decay/archival sweep. Invalidate-don't-delete is correct for auditability but MUST be paired with a decay or community-summarization or archival pass (Supermemory decay, Zep communities) or the store grows unbounded and retrieval slows. v2 already has an .archive/ curator pattern - apply it to memory.

Avoid stuffing top-k retrieved memories into every prompt. Inject only what's new/relevant for THIS turn (Supermemory's 'what's already been seen' dedup, Anthropic's smallest-high-signal-set), and clear stale tool results - or you induce context rot and pay for tokens that lower accuracy.

Avoid building a full temporal knowledge graph as the FIRST move. Zep's graph only beats full-context at long-horizon enterprise scale; for short sessions it barely wins and it adds entity-resolution cost, write amplification, and a multi-store-sync operational burden. v2 already has GBrain for the graph plane - extend bi-temporal validity onto the existing versioned store first; reach for heavy graph synthesis only when long-horizon recall demands it.

Avoid a cheap model on the WRITE/reconcile path. Memory quality is bottlenecked by the model that decides ADD-vs-UPDATE, resolves entities, and detects contradictions; using Haiku there pollutes the store. (v2's token-tiering pinned aux slots to Haiku - keep the memory-write controller on a capable model, recall/embedding can stay cheap.)

Avoid adding a new CORE memory tool that ships on every API call. Per v2's AGENTS.md idiom, the write-policy/recall-merge logic belongs in the memory plugins + aggregator + the existing memory_tool.py edge, not as new always-loaded core surface. Don't bloat the per-call tool set (Anthropic's own 'if a human can't pick the tool, neither can the agent').

Avoid per-provider scoping drift - don't let honcho, mem0, and local each invent their own user/session keying. Normalize one scope schema (user/agent/session/org) in the aggregator so a memory written under one provider is filterable the same way as another, or cross-store merge will leak or mis-scope.
