topic: Per-agent isolated memory vs shared orchestrator memory in multi-agent systems, and how to bridge them via opt-in promotion (recommendation for OpenComputer v2)
key_patterns: Isolation-by-default, summarize-then-return (Anthropic orchestrator-worker). Each subagent runs in its OWN context window and NEVER sees siblings; it acts as an 'intelligent filter' that gathers raw info then returns a CONDENSED list/summary to the lead. The lead's context only ever sees the compressed result, not the subagent's working memory. This is the canonical default and the exact pattern v2 already implements: delegate_tool.py runs children with skip_memory=True and the parent context 'only sees the delegation call and the summary result'.

Filesystem/external-store handoff to avoid the 'game of telephone' (Anthropic appendix). For heavy outputs (code, reports, large result sets), subagents write artifacts to an external store and pass only a LIGHTWEIGHT REFERENCE back to the coordinator, instead of funneling full content through conversation history. This prevents both information loss across stages and token blow-up. Maps directly to v2 having artifact storage + a reference returned in the delegate summary.

Blackboard / shared-scratchpad architecture: a central shared store ('blackboard') that specialized 'knowledge sources' (agents) read from and write to; agents do NOT message each other directly, they coordinate only through what they post. Crucially, agents keep PRIVATE knowledge (their data partition / working state) and post ONLY their responses to the shared board — a built-in promotion boundary. The LLM blackboard paper reports 13-57% end-to-end success gains and explicitly frames the core problem as 'the main agent lacks full observability over sub-agents' knowledge', which is exactly the orchestrator-situational-awareness gap.

Two-tier memory split (LangGraph): short-term = thread-scoped state via a checkpointer (per-session, per thread_id, dies on thread change); long-term = a BaseStore namespaced by identity (e.g. ('memories','user-123')) that survives across threads/sessions. Promotion = explicitly writing a distilled fact from thread state into the namespaced store. Namespaces are the isolation primitive: per-agent namespace = isolated; a shared namespace = orchestrator-visible.

Scoped-view sharing (CrewAI): default is ONE shared crew memory all agents read/write; isolation is opt-in via memory.scope('/agent/researcher') giving an agent a private subtree, plus a read-only slice pattern (an agent reads its own scope + shared company knowledge but cannot write to shared). Promotion is automatic-by-default here: 'after each task the crew extracts discrete facts from task output and stores them' into shared, then injects relevant recall into the next agent's prompt. This is the opposite default from Anthropic and is explicitly called out as having NO per-user isolation = production hazard.

Teachability / retrieve-don't-inject (AutoGen/AG2): private long-term memory ('memos') persists to a per-agent vector DB across chats; instead of copying all memos into context, memos are retrieved into context ONLY as needed. GroupChat is the shared-context counterpart: all agents subscribe/publish to one message thread (full shared situational awareness, but high token cost and cross-contamination risk). The pairing shows the same isolated-store + selective-surfacing split.

Generative-Agents consolidation pipeline (the promotion machinery): raw observations -> importance score 1-10 -> periodic REFLECTION that condenses the ~100 most recent records into a few high-level insights -> those insights are promoted to long-term memory; retrieval = recency * importance * relevance, and successfully-used memories get their relevance boosted and decay timer reset. This is the reference design for HOW to decide what gets promoted.

Consolidation four-lever framework (Hindsight, production memory vendor): Importance (write-time gate via atomic fact extraction, not raw dumps), Merge (entity resolution AT WRITE TIME with recency-wins + explicit invalidation rather than delete), Decay (exponential/Ebbinghaus for state, step-function for stable facts), Eviction (only for compliance/GDPR). Explicitly warns 'summarize-and-drop is lossy compaction, not consolidation'.
tradeoffs: Isolation gives focused context, privacy, and no cross-contamination, but costs the orchestrator situational awareness — the lead literally 'lacks full observability over sub-agents' knowledge' (blackboard paper) and cross-agent learning is lost. Sharing gives coordination + cross-agent learning + auditability but risks context pollution, token blow-up, and leakage.

Push-everything-to-shared (CrewAI default: auto-extract every task output) maximizes cross-agent learning but is the documented failure path — 'no per-user isolation, will fail fast in production' and produces an index where 'five contradictory facts about the same entity' degrade answers. Pull-on-demand (AutoGen teachability: retrieve memos only as needed) keeps context clean but the orchestrator may miss relevant prior findings it didn't think to query.

Pass-through-context (everything flows through the lead's conversation) is simplest and most transparent but hits the 200k-token truncation wall and the 'game of telephone' distortion. Filesystem/reference handoff (Anthropic) fixes both but adds a store + a retrieval step and a stale-reference risk.

Automatic promotion (write on every delegation) maximizes recall and is zero-effort for agent authors, but grows the shared store unboundedly and pollutes retrieval (near-duplicates compete for top-k, stale facts outscore current ones). Explicit/gated promotion (subagent or orchestrator decides) keeps the store clean and intentional but can silently drop useful context if the gate is too strict.

Importance scoring via an LLM call per write gives good filtering but 'adds a model call per write and ratings drift across model versions'; cheap heuristic gates (fact extraction, tags, explicit promote flag) are deterministic and free but coarser.

A single shared namespace gives the orchestrator one place to look (great awareness) but couples all agents and leaks one agent's context into another's retrieval; per-agent namespaces + an explicit shared 'promoted' namespace preserve isolation but require the orchestrator to know which namespace to query.
failure_modes: Leakage / privacy violation: a subagent's raw working memory or a user's PII surfaces to the orchestrator or to a sibling agent that should not see it. CrewAI's shared-by-default is explicitly flagged: 'no per-user isolation, will fail fast in production'. Mitigation: isolation is the default; promotion must be an explicit, redaction-aware write, never an automatic dump of full child state.

Context pollution: promoting raw/verbose subagent output into the shared store means retrieval returns 'five contradictory facts about the same entity' and 'does not produce a coherent answer even from a strong model'. Mitigation: promote only extracted atomic facts/insights (the Generative-Agents reflection + Hindsight importance lever), never raw transcripts.

Unbounded shared store + index degradation: as the shared store grows, 'more near-duplicates compete for the top-k slot and more chances for a stale fact to outscore a current one'. Mitigation: dedup/entity-resolution at write time, recency-wins-with-invalidation, exponential decay; monitor that token-cost-per-turn stays flat as the index grows.

Stale-fact dominance: promoted facts never get invalidated, so the orchestrator 'confidently tells a customer they're on Postgres six weeks after they migrated to MySQL'. Mitigation: mark superseded facts invalid (not delete), step-function decay for stable attributes, recency-wins for state.

Game of telephone: funneling everything through the lead's conversation history distorts multi-stage results and loses detail. Mitigation: write heavy outputs to a store, pass lightweight references back (Anthropic).

Entity fragmentation: the same entity promoted under different names ('Ben' vs 'Ben Bartholomew') fragments retrieval and loses cross-session context. Mitigation: entity resolution at write/promote time, not query time.

Token/coordination blowup from full sharing (AutoGen GroupChat broadcasts every message to every agent) — scales badly and re-introduces cross-contamination. Mitigation: don't make sub-agent memory globally broadcast; promote selectively to a scoped shared namespace.

Over-aggressive promotion gate: 'useful context disappears before it can be retrieved' if the importance filter is too strict. Mitigation: tune the gate, prefer fact-extraction filtering over hard 1-10 thresholds, and keep an audit trail of what was/ wasn't promoted.
adopt: Keep isolated-by-default. v2 already does the right thing: subagents run with skip_memory=True (no provider session, own context window) and the parent only sees the delegate summary. Do NOT switch to CrewAI-style auto-share-everything. Document this as the intended default in AGENTS.md.

Make promotion an OPT-IN write through the EXISTING on_delegation seam. agent/memory_provider.py already defines on_delegation(task, result, child_session_id) as 'parent-side observation of subagent work', and tools/delegate_tool.py:2638 already calls memory_manager.on_delegation(task=goal, result=summary, child_session_id=...). The bridge is: have the active provider's on_delegation OPTIONALLY write a distilled record into the parent/orchestrator's store, gated by a config flag (default OFF). Zero new core tool, zero schema change — this is the v2-idiom 'capability at the edges' path.

Promote only the CONDENSED summary, never raw child memory. The result passed to on_delegation is already entry['summary'] (the compressed child output), so promotion stays leak-safe and pollution-safe by construction. Before writing, run it through the existing memory write-path's threat/injection scan (v2 already has build_memory_context_block threat-scan fence) and extract atomic facts rather than storing the whole blob.

Add a default-OFF config gate, e.g. memory.promote_subagent_results in config.yaml with modes {off (default) | explicit | summarize | auto}: off = never; explicit = only when the subagent emits a promote marker / writes to a designated 'shared' scope; summarize = run a cheap extraction over the delegate summary then write atomic facts; auto = write the summary verbatim (discouraged, for power users). Per the .env-is-secrets-only rule, this is behavioral config so it belongs in config.yaml.

Implement an explicit hand-back primitive: give subagents a way to mark specific findings 'promote to orchestrator' (a tag/section in their final response, or a tiny memory_promote action), so promotion is intentional. This mirrors the blackboard 'agents post only their responses, keep private knowledge private' boundary and Anthropic's 'return a list, not raw findings'.

Dedup + recency-wins-with-invalidation on promote. Before writing a promoted fact to the shared store, do write-time entity resolution and mark any superseded fact invalid rather than deleting (Hindsight merge lever). For v2's local SQLite store this is a simple key/entity match + a tombstone column.

Use namespacing for the store layout: per-agent/per-session namespace for isolated memory (already the model) + a single explicit 'orchestrator/shared' namespace that promotion writes into and that ONLY the orchestrator prefetches. This keeps the orchestrator's situational-awareness query cheap and bounded, and keeps siblings from reading each other.

For heavy subagent outputs, promote a REFERENCE (artifact id / file path) not the content — v2 already has the artifact registry + SSE artifact.created; pass the artifact id through the delegate summary and let the orchestrator dereference on demand. This is Anthropic's filesystem-handoff verbatim.

Add bounded growth + decay to the shared promoted store: a max-size / TTL / exponential-decay on promoted items, and a metric (token-cost-per-turn flat as index grows) so promotion can't silently balloon. Reuse the existing memory garbage-filter/dedup that already trims the Memory tab 86->48.
avoid: Do NOT auto-promote every subagent result into a global shared store (CrewAi default). It is the documented production failure ('no per-user isolation, fail fast') and pollutes retrieval with contradictions and near-duplicates.
Do NOT broadcast subagent memory to all agents (AutoGen GroupChat style) — it re-introduces cross-contamination and token blowup that isolation was protecting against.

Do NOT promote raw child transcripts or the child's working memory — only extracted atomic facts / the already-condensed summary. 'Summarize-and-drop' of raw context is lossy compaction, not consolidation.
Do NOT add a new core tool or change the tool schema for this — every core tool ships on every API call. Use the existing on_delegation hook + provider write-path; gate behind config.
Do NOT skip write-time dedup/entity-resolution and rely on query-time fixes — that 'fragments the index and slows every retrieval', and lets stale facts outscore current ones.
Do NOT make promotion default-ON. Default isolated; opt-in only, matching v2's pattern of shipping new behavior default-OFF (dreaming, self-evolution loop).
Do NOT use a per-write LLM importance score as the only gate (cost + version drift); prefer fact-extraction + an explicit promote marker, with optional LLM scoring as a power-user mode.
sources: https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/built-multi-agent-research-system — Anthropic's primary production write-up; canonical orchestrator-worker memory: lead saves plan to Memory past 200k tokens, subagents as 'intelligent filters' returning condensed lists, filesystem handoff to avoid 'game of telephone', lightweight references vs full results.

https://docs.crewai.com/en/concepts/memory — CrewAI official docs; the shared-by-default + opt-in scope('/agent/...') + read-only-slice model, auto fact-extraction promotion, reset/scope semantics. Primary framework source for the share-by-default pole.

https://docs.ag2.ai/latest/docs/user-guide/advanced-concepts/groupchat/groupchat/ + AutoGen teachability docs (microsoft.github.io/autogen/0.2/docs/notebooks/agentchat_teachability) — primary framework sources for GroupChat shared-thread vs per-agent teachable memos with retrieve-on-demand.

https://arxiv.org/pdf/2304.03442 — Park et al. 'Generative Agents'; the importance(1-10) + reflection-condenses-recent-records + recency*importance*relevance retrieval consolidation pipeline (the promotion machinery reference).

topic: How teams evaluate agent/long-term memory systems (benchmarks, retrieval-quality metrics, write quality, latency/cost, regression testing) — and a concrete minimal eval design for OpenComputer v2
key_patterns: END-TO-END QA ACCURACY via LLM-as-judge is the headline metric across every production memory system. LoCoMo (1,540 Qs, 5 types: single-hop, multi-hop, temporal, open-domain, adversarial), LongMemEval (500 Qs, 6 categories incl. knowledge-update and abstention), and Mem0's BEAM (1M/10M tokens, 10 categories) all score correctness by feeding the model+memory a question, then a SEPARATE stronger judge LLM labels the answer CORRECT/WRONG against a gold answer ('touches on the same topic as the gold answer'). Mem0 reports this as the 'J score'; LongMemEval writes an autoeval_label via GPT-4o. F1 and BLEU-1 are reported alongside but the papers explicitly call them weak for factual accuracy.

STRATIFY BY QUESTION TYPE, never just one aggregate number. Every system breaks accuracy out by single-hop / multi-hop / temporal / open-domain (+ knowledge-update + abstention). This is load-bearing: Mem0's headline gains were '+29.6 on temporal, +23.1 on multi-hop' — an aggregate would have hidden where the win was. Temporal and multi-hop are where naive memory fails.

RETRIEVAL IS A SEPARATE, CHEAPER EVAL LAYER measured with rank metrics on a gold set, independent of generation. Standard set: Recall@k (fraction of relevant facts in top-k), Precision@k, Hit-Rate@k (>=1 relevant in top-k), MRR (1/rank of first relevant, rewards early hits), nDCG@k (rank-aware, graded relevance; BEIR's primary metric is nDCG@10). LongMemEval evaluates retrieval at TWO granularities: turn-level recall (has_answer:true labels on turns) and session-level recall (answer_session_ids the system should retrieve). This catches recall regressions deterministically without paying for an LLM judge on every run.

GOLD-SET CONSTRUCTION = fixed (fact_to_store, query, expected_fact_ids) triples. Build a frozen corpus of memory items, a frozen set of queries, and for each query a hand-labeled set of which stored items are 'relevant'. This is the BEIR pattern (corpus + queries + qrels relevance judgements). Once frozen and checked into the repo, retrieval metrics become deterministic and diffable across commits — the recall regression gate.

WRITE-QUALITY is measured separately from read-quality and is where most systems silently rot. Four write checks: (a) right-fact-stored — after ingesting a turn, assert the target fact is retrievable; (b) dedup rate — store the same fact N times, assert one row / same id (v2's MemoryStore already dedups by content UNIQUE constraint, add_fact returns the existing id); (c) contradiction/knowledge-update — store fact, then store a superseding fact, assert the NEW value wins on retrieval (LongMemEval's 'knowledge update' category; the v2 test test_fact_update_returns_latest already does this); (d) abstention — query for something never stored, assert the system returns nothing / 'I don't know' rather than hallucinating (LongMemEval's abstention category, ~30 instances). Mem0's blog flags the failure mode directly: 'dedup only blocks identical open triples; conflicting facts accumulate silently; no contradiction detection.'

LATENCY + TOKEN COST ARE FIRST-CLASS PASS/FAIL METRICS, reported as p50/p95, not averages. Mem0 paper: search latency p50/p95 = 0.148s/0.200s; total response p50/p95 = 0.708s/1.440s vs full-context 9.87s/17.12s (a 91-92% p95 cut). Token budget is the production gate: Mem0 holds ~6,900-7,000 tokens/query vs full-context ~26,000; their rule is 'a system that scores well on accuracy but requires 26,000 tokens per query is not production-viable.' Measure tokens-injected-per-recall and per-turn recall latency as budgets that fail the build when exceeded.

STATISTICAL RIGOR: report the mean of ~10 independent runs with variance (±), not a single run. The Zep/Mem0 LoCoMo dispute hinged on this — Zep's claimed 84% collapsed to 58.44%±0.20 once (a) adversarial questions were handled consistently in numerator AND denominator, (b) all systems used identical prompts/retrieval templates, and (c) results were averaged over 10 runs instead of 1. LLM-judge scores carry a ±1-point confidence interval from judge inconsistency, so single runs are noise.

SYNTHETIC NEEDLE-IN-HAYSTACK (RULER pattern) for stress-testing retrieval robustness: RULER generates configurable-length contexts with planted 'needles' and tests Single/Multi-key/Multi-value/Multi-query NIAH plus variable-tracking (multi-hop) and aggregation. The transferable idea for a memory eval: plant a known fact among M distractor facts and assert it's retrieved at increasing corpus sizes — measures how recall degrades as the store grows, which a fixed small gold set won't reveal.

USE A DIFFERENT JUDGE MODEL THAN THE ONE IN PRODUCTION and aggregate over the dataset — per-trajectory LLM-judge scores are noisy. This is already encoded in v2's agent-eval SKILL.md ('Use a different model to judge than the one in production... aggregate over a dataset — per-trajectory scores are noisy').
tradeoffs: LLM-as-judge (captures semantic correctness, the only metric that tracks real quality) vs deterministic retrieval metrics (recall@k/MRR/nDCG — free, fast, fully reproducible, diffable, but only score retrieval not the final answer). Resolution every team converges on: BOTH layers — cheap deterministic retrieval metrics on every CI run as the fast regression gate, expensive LLM-judge QA accuracy on a nightly/release cadence.

Frozen gold set (deterministic, regression-safe, cheap) vs generic public benchmark like LoCoMo (comparable to literature but Mem0 explicitly warns '91.6 on LoCoMo does not tell you how the system performs on your healthcare or legal workload'). Resolution: a small repo-local frozen gold set for the CI gate, optionally LoCoMo for external comparability.

Token efficiency vs accuracy: full-context dumping maximizes accuracy ceiling but costs ~26k tokens/query and 17s p95 — non-viable in production. Selective retrieval (~7k tokens, 1.4s p95) trades a few accuracy points for 4x cost and ~10x latency wins. The eval must report BOTH axes so a token-blowup regression is visible even when accuracy looks fine.

Async writes (Mem0's default in v1.0.0, avoids blocking response generation — 'the most common production footgun') vs sync writes (simpler, but the write latency lands on the user's turn). If writes are async, the eval must separately measure write-completion latency and a write-then-immediately-read race, because async writes can make a fact briefly un-retrievable.

Recall@k with larger k (more forgiving, higher recall, but injects more tokens and more distractors into the reader) vs small k (tighter token budget, but misses relevant facts). Report recall as a curve over k (k=1,3,5,10) rather than a single k so the precision/recall/token trade is explicit; pair with reranking (Mem0 ships a second-pass reranker) to keep small-k precision high.
failure_modes: Single aggregate accuracy number hides per-type regressions — temporal and multi-hop fail independently of single-hop. Always stratify.

Inconsistent denominators / mixing categories — the exact Zep error: counting adversarial/abstention questions in the numerator but not the denominator inflated 58% to 84%. Define the question set and the pass/fail rule per category up front and never deviate.

Single-run LLM-judge scores treated as ground truth — judge has ±1pt noise; one run is indistinguishable from a real regression. Average >=5-10 runs with variance, or use deterministic retrieval metrics as the gate and LLM-judge only as a periodic signal.
Judge == production model — self-grading inflates scores; v2's own skill already forbids this.

Silent contradiction accumulation — dedup that only blocks byte-identical content lets 'married_to=A' and 'married_to=B' coexist; without a knowledge-update test, retrieval starts returning stale facts and nothing flags it. (v2's add_fact dedups only by exact content UNIQUE constraint — so semantically-contradicting facts WILL coexist; the eval must include a knowledge-update case to surface this.)

No abstention test — a memory that confidently fabricates an answer when nothing was stored passes every recall test but is dangerous. LongMemEval makes abstention a first-class category for exactly this reason.

Optimizing accuracy while token cost silently balloons — a retrieval change that 'helps accuracy' by injecting 4x more context passes an accuracy-only gate but kills the production cost/latency budget. Gate on tokens-per-recall and p95 latency too.

FTS5 implicit-AND gotcha (v2-specific): MemoryStore.search_facts uses FTS5 MATCH which ANDs query terms, so a natural-language query with a word absent from the stored content returns nothing — recall measured with NL queries will look catastrophically low vs keyword queries. The existing v2 recall_probe.py already documented this (FTS5 recall 0.62 NL vs 1.00 OR). The gold set must fix the query style or the eval measures the query encoder, not the store.

Eval store not isolated per run — reusing a DB across cases means earlier writes pollute later retrievals and dedup/contradiction results become order-dependent. Use a tmp_path DB per case (the v2 test fixture pattern).
adopt: Build `tests/tools/test_memory_recall_eval.py` (or a `skills/memory-eval/` skill mirroring agent-eval) that drives the existing `plugins.memory.holographic.store.MemoryStore` directly — it already exposes add_fact (dedup), search_facts (returns fact_id-ordered ranked list — ready for recall@k/MRR/nDCG), update_fact, record_feedback. No new core tool needed; this is an edge eval over an existing plugin, the v2 way.

Freeze a repo-local GOLD SET as YAML: a `corpus` of facts to store, and `queries`, each query carrying `relevant_fact_ids` (BEIR-style qrels). Suggested minimal size to start: ~30-50 facts, ~20 queries spanning the LongMemEval categories — single-hop, multi-hop, temporal, knowledge-update, and abstention (query with empty relevant set). Check it into the repo so retrieval metrics are deterministic and diffable.

Compute and report deterministic retrieval metrics from search_facts output: Recall@{1,3,5,10}, Precision@k, Hit-Rate@k, MRR, nDCG@10. These need no LLM and run in milliseconds — make them the per-commit CI gate (fail if Recall@5 or MRR drops below a frozen threshold, e.g. recall@5 >= 0.90 on the gold set).

Add WRITE-QUALITY cases reusing the patterns already in test_memory_layer_verify.py: (a) right-fact-stored = ingest then assert retrievable; (b) dedup = add_fact same content twice, assert same id (already proven); (c) knowledge-update/contradiction = store fact, store superseding fact, assert NEW value ranks above old on retrieval (extend the existing test_fact_update_returns_latest — make it ASSERT the new value outranks the old, not just that it's present); (d) abstention = query for never-stored content, assert empty/low-trust result.

Measure latency + token budget as gates: wrap search_facts in a timer, record p50/p95 search latency over the query set, and estimate injected-token count (sum tokens of returned facts at the chosen k). Fail the gate on regressions vs frozen budgets — borrow Mem0's framing (p95 recall latency and tokens-per-recall as named budgets, not averages).

Run each metric over N>=5 repetitions (search_facts has a retrieval_count side-effect but ranking is deterministic; repetition matters once you add any LLM-judge layer) and report mean ± std. When/if you add an end-to-end LLM-judge accuracy layer, use a judge model DIFFERENT from the production model and average over the dataset (already mandated by agent-eval SKILL.md).

Wire into CI exactly like agent-eval: a job that runs the memory eval and exits non-zero when case pass-rate or any frozen retrieval threshold drops — mirror `python eval.py --cases ... --threshold 0.9 || exit 1`. Emit a `--json` machine-readable report (per-query recall@k, MRR, nDCG, latency p95, tokens) so regressions are inspectable. Make the retrieval-metric gate the cheap always-on layer; keep the LLM-judge QA layer as a separate nightly/release job.

Pin the query STYLE in the gold set to match the store's retriever (FTS5 keyword semantics for the holographic provider) OR explicitly test the NL→keyword gap as its own metric — the existing recall_probe.py already quantified this (0.62 NL vs 1.00 OR); fold that probe's gold cases into the frozen set so the FTS5 implicit-AND failure mode can't silently return.

Add a synthetic scale-stress case (RULER-style): plant one known fact among M generated distractors, sweep M (e.g. 10/100/1000) and assert recall@5 stays above threshold — measures how recall degrades as the store grows, which the small fixed gold set won't catch.
avoid: Don't ship a single aggregate accuracy number — it hides per-type and recall regressions. Stratify by question type.
Don't rely on F1/BLEU for factual correctness — the Mem0 paper explicitly calls them weak; they're report-only companions to the judge score, never the gate.
Don't gate on single-run LLM-judge scores (±1pt noise) — use deterministic retrieval metrics for the per-commit gate and average LLM-judge over >=5 runs for the periodic signal.
Don't let the production model judge its own answers.
Don't add a new CORE memory-eval tool — every core tool ships on every API call (v2 AGENTS.md rule). This is an edge eval: a test file or a skill over the existing memory plugin.
Don't reuse one DB across eval cases — isolate per case (tmp_path) or dedup/contradiction results become order-dependent.

Don't measure recall with natural-language queries against an FTS5 keyword store without acknowledging the implicit-AND gap — you'll measure the query phrasing, not the memory. (v2-specific, already burned once per recall_probe.py.)
Don't gate on accuracy alone while ignoring tokens-per-recall and p95 latency — a 'helpful' retrieval change can quietly 4x the cost and pass an accuracy-only gate.

Don't import LoCoMo/LongMemEval wholesale as the CI gate — Mem0 warns generic benchmark scores don't predict your workload; use a small repo-local frozen gold set for the gate, public benchmarks only for external comparison.
